title: Scaling Properties of Continuous Diffusion Spoken Language Models
gist: Apple/Google researchers show continuous diffusion (CD) spoken language models are a viable alternative to discrete autoregressive SLMs, exhibiting clean scaling laws and, at 16B parameters trained on tens of millions of hours of speech, generating emotive, multi-speaker, multilingual audio though long-form coherence stays unsolved.

insights.claim: Speech-only spoken language models still lag text and text-speech models on performance, and recent discrete autoregressive SLMs need heavy compute and data just to approach text-model quality.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Voice-native LLMs are behind text; the compute/data cost of the discrete AR path is a bottleneck to watch for anyone betting on on-device speech AI.
insights.quote: Speech-only spoken language models (SLMs) lag behind text and text-speech models in performance, with recent discrete autoregressive (AR) SLMs indicating significant computational and data demands to match text models.
insights.locator: Speech-only spoken language models (SLMs) lag behind

insights.claim: Discretizing continuous speech for autoregressive modeling creates bottlenecks, which is the specific motivation for testing continuous diffusion as a more viable modeling approach.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: The tokenization bottleneck is the crux; a diffusion path that sidesteps it could reset the cost curve for speech models.
insights.quote: Since discretizing continuous speech for AR creates bottlenecks, we explore whether continuous diffusion (CD) SLM is more viable.
insights.locator: Since discretizing continuous speech for AR creates bottlenecks

insights.claim: Continuous diffusion SLMs exhibit clean scaling laws for both validation loss and the phoneme Jensen-Shannon divergence metric, mirroring the scaling behavior of autoregressive models.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Predictable scaling laws mean CD SLM investment can be forecast and de-risked the same way AR/text-model scaling has been.
insights.quote: Our analysis reveals CD SLMs, mirroring AR behavior, exhibit scaling laws for validation loss and pJSD, and show optimal token-to-parameter ratios decreasing as compute scales.
insights.locator: Our analysis reveals CD SLMs, mirroring AR behavior

insights.claim: The compute-optimal token-to-parameter ratio for continuous diffusion SLMs decreases as compute scales, meaning larger-compute runs favor relatively more parameters per token.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Guides how to allocate a fixed speech-training budget between model size and data as compute grows.
insights.locator: show optimal token-to-parameter ratios decreasing as compute scales

insights.claim: At high compute, continuous diffusion SLM loss becomes insensitive to the specific choice of data and model sizes, signaling potential for fast inference.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Insensitivity to allocation lets teams pick smaller/faster models near optimum, a lever for cheaper on-device speech inference.
insights.quote: for the latter, loss becomes insensitive to choice of data and model sizes, showing potential for fast inference.
insights.locator: However, for the latter, loss becomes insensitive

insights.claim: As compute increases the curvature of isoFLOP curves at their optima flattens, expanding by roughly two orders of magnitude the range of model and dataset sizes that still land within epsilon of optimal loss, opening an efficient inference frontier.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A wide near-optimal frontier means practitioners can trade model size for inference speed with little accuracy loss, key for deployment economics.
insights.quote: flattening corresponds to approximately two orders of magnitude expansion in the range of model (ΔN) and dataset (ΔD) sizes yielding a loss within ε of the optimum L*
insights.locator: The curvature κ of isoFLOPs at their optima decreases

insights.claim: Scaling continuous diffusion SLMs to 16B parameters trained on tens of millions of hours of conversational data enables generation of emotive, prosodic, multi-speaker, multilingual speech.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the data/compute scale (16B params, tens of millions of speech-hours) required to reach expressive multi-speaker voice generation.
insights.quote: Scaling CD SLMs to 16B parameters with tens of millions of hours of conversational data enables generation of emotive, prosodic, multi-speaker, multilingual speech

insights.claim: Long-form coherence remains a significant unsolved challenge even for 16B-parameter continuous diffusion spoken language models.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: The gating limitation for real products like long dialogue or audiobooks; the milestone to watch before speech LLMs are deployment-ready.
insights.quote: though achieving long-form coherence remains a significant challenge
insights.locator: though achieving long-form coherence remains a significant challenge

insights.claim: The authors introduce the phoneme Jensen-Shannon divergence (pJSD) as a metric to quantify the linguistic quality of spoken language models.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A new standardized speech-quality metric could become a benchmark others adopt, useful for tracking competitive progress in voice models.
insights.quote: To quantify the SLMs linguistic quality, we introduce the phoneme Jensen-Shannon divergence (pJSD) metric.
insights.locator: To quantify the SLMs linguistic quality

insights.claim: The paper's continuous diffusion speech work was done while several authors were at Apple, but core contributors including Jason Ramapuram and Bogdan Mazoure are marked as being at Google.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Signals speech-AI talent flow from Apple toward Google, a competitive-positioning tell for who is building next-gen voice models.
insights.quote: † Google‡ Core contributor§ Core advising** Work done while at Apple
